description: Dynamic equilibrium, equilibrium constant (Keq), reaction quotient (Q), Le Châtelier's principle, and ICE table calculations.

problems.problem: What is dynamic equilibrium? Describe the characteristics of a system at equilibrium.

step_by_step.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium occurs when the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction.
Concentrations of reactants and products remain constant (but not necessarily equal). Reactions continue at the molecular level. The system is closed.

problems.final_answer: Dynamic equilibrium: forward rate = reverse rate; concentrations constant; reactions continue microscopically

problems.problem: Write the equilibrium expression for the reaction: $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$.

step_by_step.explanation: $K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{products}]^{\text{coefficients}}}{[\text{reactants}]^{\text{coefficients}}}$

problems.problem: Write the equilibrium expression for the reaction: $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$.

step_by_step.explanation: $K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{products}]^{\text{coefficients}}}{[\text{reactants}]^{\text{coefficients}}}$

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$, the equilibrium concentrations are $[\text{H}_2] = 0.10\ \text{M}$, $[\text{I}_2] = 0.20\ \text{M}$, and $[\text{HI}] = 0.40\ \text{M}$. Calculate $K_{eq}$.

For the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 4.0$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. If $[\text{NO}_2] = 0.50\ \text{M}$ at equilibrium, what is $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]$?

For the reaction $2\text{NO}(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NOCl}(g)$, $K_{eq} = 0.50$ at a certain temperature. If $[\text{NO}] = 0.20\ \text{M}$ and $[\text{Cl}_2] = 0.10\ \text{M}$ at equilibrium, calculate $[\text{NOCl}]$.
Define the reaction quotient $Q$. How does comparing $Q$ to $K_{eq}$ tell you the direction a reaction will proceed?

step_by_step.explanation: $Q$ is calculated the same way as $K_{eq}$ but using current concentrations, not necessarily equilibrium concentrations.
If $Q < K_{eq}$, reaction proceeds forward (toward products). If $Q > K_{eq}$, reaction proceeds reverse (toward reactants). If $Q = K_{eq}$, system is at equilibrium.

problems.final_answer: $Q = \frac{[\text{products}]^{\text{coeff}}}{[\text{reactants}]^{\text{coeff}}}$; $Q < K_{eq} \rightarrow$ forward; $Q > K_{eq} \rightarrow$ reverse; $Q = K_{eq} \rightarrow$ equilibrium

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$, $K_{eq} = 54.0$ at a given temperature. If $[\text{H}_2] = 0.10\ \text{M}$, $[\text{I}_2] = 0.20\ \text{M}$, and $[\text{HI}] = 0.50\ \text{M}$, determine the direction the reaction will proceed.

step_by_step.explanation: $Q < K_{eq}$, so reaction proceeds forward (toward products)

problems.problem: State Le Châtelier's principle.

step_by_step.explanation: When a system at equilibrium is subjected to a stress, the equilibrium will shift in the direction that partially relieves the stress.

problems.final_answer: Le Châtelier's principle: equilibrium shifts to counteract applied stress

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ (exothermic), predict the effect of increasing $[\text{H}_2]$ on the equilibrium position.

step_by_step.explanation: Increasing concentration of a reactant ($\text{H}_2$)
System will shift to consume the added reactant

step_by_step.calculation: Equilibrium shifts to the right (toward products, $\text{NH}_3$)

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ (exothermic), predict the effect of removing $\text{NH}_3$ on the equilibrium position.

step_by_step.explanation: Removing a product ($\text{NH}_3$)
System will shift to produce more product

step_by_step.calculation: Equilibrium shifts to the right (toward products)

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ (exothermic), predict the effect of increasing temperature on the equilibrium position.

step_by_step.explanation: Increasing temperature adds heat
Exothermic reaction releases heat, so heat is a product
Adding heat (product) shifts equilibrium toward reactants

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, predict the effect of decreasing the volume of the container on the equilibrium position.

step_by_step.explanation: Decreasing volume increases pressure

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants: 4 moles gas ($1 + 3$), Products: 2 moles gas

step_by_step.explanation: System shifts toward fewer moles of gas to reduce pressure

step_by_step.calculation: Equilibrium shifts right (toward $\text{NH}_3$, fewer gas moles)

problems.problem: For the reaction $2\text{NO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g)$ (colorless), where $\text{NO}_2$ is brown, predict the color change when pressure is increased.

step_by_step.explanation: Increasing pressure (decreasing volume)
System shifts toward fewer moles of gas
Shift right produces colorless $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$, so brown color fades

problems.final_answer: Brown color fades as equilibrium shifts toward colorless $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4$

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)$, $K_{eq} = 54.0$. Initially, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.50\ \text{M}$, $[\text{I}_2] = 0.50\ \text{M}$, and $[\text{HI}] = 0\ \text{M}$. Calculate the equilibrium concentrations.

For the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 0.50$. Initially, $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = 1.00\ \text{M}$ and $[\text{NO}_2] = 0\ \text{M}$. Calculate the equilibrium concentrations.

For the reaction $2\text{HI}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 0.020$. If $2.00\ \text{mol}$ of $\text{HI}$ is placed in a $1.00\ \text{L}$ container, calculate the equilibrium concentrations of all species.

For the reaction $\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 0.0300$ at $200^{\circ}\text{C}$. If $0.500\ \text{mol}$ of $\text{PCl}_5$ is placed in a $2.00\ \text{L}$ container, calculate the equilibrium concentration of $\text{Cl}_2$.

For the reaction $\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 10.0$. Initially, $[\text{CO}] = 0.100\ \text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2\text{O}] = 0.100\ \text{M}$, $[\text{CO}_2] = 0\ \text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0\ \text{M}$. Calculate the equilibrium concentration of $\text{CO}_2$.

For the reaction $2\text{NH}_3(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 0.500$. Initially, $[\text{NH}_3] = 1.00\ \text{M}$, $[\text{N}_2] = 0\ \text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0\ \text{M}$. Calculate the equilibrium concentration of $\text{NH}_3$.

step_by_step.step: Set equal to 0.500 and take square root

problems.problem: Write the equilibrium expression for: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Pure solids and liquids are excluded from $K_{eq}$ expressions because their concentrations are constant.
Only $\text{CO}_2(g)$ is a gas; $\text{CaCO}_3$ and $\text{CaO}$ are solids.

problems.problem: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, $K_c = 0.50$ at $400\ \text{K}$. Calculate $K_p$. ($R = 0.08206\ \text{L}\cdot\text{atm/mol}\cdot\text{K}$)

For $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$, $K_{eq} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$. Initially, $[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = 0.50\ \text{M}$. Calculate $[\text{NO}_2]$ at equilibrium using the small $x$ approximation.

A $0.100\ \text{M}$ solution of weak acid $\text{HA}$ dissociates according to $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$. At equilibrium, $[\text{H}^+] = 1.34 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{M}$. Calculate the percent dissociation.
For $2\text{SO}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(g)$ ($\Delta H < 0$), predict the effect on $[\text{SO}_3]$ if: (a) temperature is increased, AND (b) volume is decreased.

step_by_step.explanation: Exothermic reaction; adding heat shifts equilibrium left $\rightarrow$ decreases $[\text{SO}_3]$
Decreasing volume increases pressure; shift toward fewer gas moles ($3 \rightarrow 2$) $\rightarrow$ shifts right $\rightarrow$ increases $[\text{SO}_3]$
Opposing stresses; net effect depends on magnitude of each change. Cannot predict direction without quantitative data.

problems.final_answer: Opposing effects: temperature increase decreases $[\text{SO}_3]$; volume decrease increases $[\text{SO}_3]$; net effect indeterminate

problems.problem: The Haber process: $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ ($\Delta H < 0$) is run at high pressure and moderate temperature. Explain why these conditions maximize $\text{NH}_3$ yield using Le Châtelier's principle.

step_by_step.explanation: 4 moles gas $\rightarrow$ 2 moles gas; high pressure shifts equilibrium right toward $\text{NH}_3$
Exothermic reaction; low temperature favors products, but too low slows reaction rate
Moderate temperature ($\sim 450^{\circ}\text{C}$) balances yield (thermodynamics) with reaction rate (kinetics); catalyst further increases rate without affecting equilibrium

problems.final_answer: High pressure favors $\text{NH}_3$ (fewer gas moles); moderate temperature balances yield and rate; catalyst speeds attainment of equilibrium
